questions.question: An individual who is not a member of either House of Parliament may be appointed to the Council of Ministers; however, they must become a member of either House within a specified time frame of:
questions.solution:
Membership of Parliament for Ministers

Under Article 75(5), a person can be appointed as a Minister even if they aren't a member of either House. However, they must secure a seat in the Rajya Sabha or Lok Sabha within 06 months of their appointment. If they fail to do so, they automatically cease to be a Minister.

questions.question: Article 23 of the Indian Constitution prohibits which of the following?
questions.option_1: Prohibition of discrimination on grounds of religion, race, caste, sex or place of birth
questions.option_2: Prohibition of solemnisation of child marriages
questions.option_3: Prohibition of traffic in human beings and forced labour
questions.option_4: Prohibition of employment of children in factories, etc.
questions.solution:
Article 23: Right against Exploitation

Article 23 prohibits traffic in human beings, 'begar' (unpaid labor), and other similar forms of forced labor. Any contravention of this provision is an offence punishable in accordance with the law. (Note: Article 24 specifically deals with child labor in factories).

questions.question: In 1964, who chaired the Committee on Prevention of Corruption and suggested the Central Vigilance Commission be established?
questions.solution:
The Santhanam Committee

The Committee on Prevention of Corruption (1962–1964), chaired by K. Santhanam, was instrumental in shaping India's anti-corruption landscape. Its recommendations led to the establishment of the Central Vigilance Commission (CVC) in 1964 to advise and guide Central Government agencies in the field of vigilance.

questions.question: In which year was the first national Census of India carried out during British India?
questions.solution:
First National Census (1871)

The first non-synchronous national Census of India was carried out in 1871-72 under the Viceroyalty of Lord Mayo. However, the first synchronous (simultaneous across the country) census was later conducted in 1881 by Lord Ripon.

questions.question: Open Market Operations (OMOs) are conducted by RBI to control ____________.
questions.solution:
Open Market Operations (OMOs)

OMOs involve the simultaneous purchase and sale of government securities by the Reserve Bank of India (RBI). The primary goal is to regulate the money supply and liquidity in the economy. When RBI buys securities, it injects cash into the system; when it sells, it absorbs excess liquidity.

questions.question: The 44ᵗʰ Amendment Act of 1978 restored the standard term of the Lok Sabha and State Legislature to how many years?
questions.solution:
Restoration of Terms (44th Amendment)

During the Emergency, the 42nd Amendment had extended the term of the Lok Sabha and State Legislative Assemblies from five to six years. The 44th Amendment Act (1978) corrected this, restoring the term back to five years.

questions.question: What is the constitutional consequence if the Lok Sabha passes a no-confidence motion against the ruling government?
questions.option_1: Only the Prime Minister must submit resignation
questions.option_2: The Council of Ministers is obligated to step down
questions.option_3: The Speaker of Lok Sabha is removed by the President
questions.option_4: The government may continue without any change
questions.solution:
Consequence of No-Confidence Motion

If the Lok Sabha passes a no-confidence motion, it indicates that the executive has lost the support of the house. Due to the principle of collective responsibility, the entire Council of Ministers, including the Prime Minister, must resign immediately.

questions.question: What is the correct sequence in which the oath of office binds a Union Minister?
questions.option_1: Integrity → Constitution → Sovereignty → Duty → Impartiality (justice without fear or favour)
questions.option_3: Allegiance to the → Constitution, → Sovereignty and integrity → Faithful discharge of duties→ Impartiality
questions.option_4: Allegiance → Sovereignty → Duty and Impartiality→ Faithful discharge
questions.solution:
The Oath of Office

The Third Schedule specifies the oath for Union Ministers. The correct sequence binds them to: 1. True faith and allegiance to the Constitution, 2. Uphold the sovereignty and integrity of India, 3. Faithful discharge of duties, and 4. Do right to all manner of people without fear or favour (Impartiality).

questions.question: What is the purpose of the Draft National Sports Governance Bill, 2024, introduced by the Indian Government?
questions.option_1: To establish a framework for ethical governance and athlete welfare in sports federations
questions.option_2: To increase commercialization of sports in Urban and Semiurban areas
questions.option_3: To centralize all sports decisions under one authority
questions.option_4: To eliminate traditional sports from national competitions
questions.solution:
Draft National Sports Governance Bill, 2024

This bill aims to overhaul the management of sports in India. Its primary focus is to establish a framework for ethical governance, ensure the welfare of athletes, and bring transparency to the functioning of National Sports Federations (NSFs).

questions.question: Which Fundamental Duty was added by the 86ᵗʰ Constitutional Amendment in 2002?
questions.option_1: To provide opportunities for education to children aged 6–14 years
questions.option_2: To promote harmony among all people of India
questions.option_3: To uphold and protect the sovereignty of India
questions.solution:
The 11th Fundamental Duty

The 86th Amendment Act (2002) introduced Article 51A(k), making it a Fundamental Duty for a parent or guardian to provide opportunities for education to his child or ward between the age of 6 and 14 years.

questions.question: Which of the following articles is INCORRECTY matched with the Constitutional Provisions related to Parliament ?
questions.option_1: Article 99 ---Composition of the Council of States/Rajya Sabha
questions.option_2: Article 93 ----Speaker and Deputy Speaker of the House of People
questions.option_3: Article 89---- Chairman and Deputy Chairman of Council of States
questions.option_4: Article 105--- Powers and Privileges of the Houses of Parliament
questions.solution:
Article 99: Oath or Affirmation

Article 99 is incorrectly matched here; it deals with the oath or affirmation by members of Parliament. The Composition of the Council of States (Rajya Sabha) is actually covered under Article 80.

questions.question: Which of the following bodies is described as the "real executive" in the Indian political system?
questions.solution:
The Real Executive

While the President is the 'Nominal Executive' (Head of State), the Prime Minister is considered the 'Real Executive' (Head of Government) because they exercise the actual powers of the executive through the Council of Ministers.

questions.question: Which of the following objectives is stated in Article 38 of the Indian Constitution?
questions.option_1: To establish a social order based on justice—social, economic, and political
questions.option_2: To guarantee employment to all citizens
questions.option_3: To provide equal education opportunities for all
questions.option_4: To establish military rule for national security
questions.solution:
Article 38: Welfare of the People

Article 38 directs the State to secure a social order for the promotion of the welfare of the people. It emphasizes the attainment of social, economic, and political justice and directs the State to minimize inequalities in income, status, and facilities.

questions.question: Which of the following provisions in the Indian Constitution deals with the duty of the State to separate the judiciary from the executive?
questions.solution:
Article 50: Separation of Powers

Article 50, a Directive Principle, mandates the State to separate the judiciary from the executive in the public services of the State. This is crucial for maintaining judicial independence and preventing executive interference in legal matters.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about Parliamentary Committees is TRUE?
questions.option_1: There are no temporary Parliamentary Committees.
questions.option_3: Ad Hoc Committees are permanent in nature.
questions.option_4: Standing Committees are reconstituted from time to time and are permanent.
questions.solution:
Types of Parliamentary Committees

Parliamentary Committees are of two kinds: Standing Committees and Ad Hoc Committees. Standing Committees are permanent in nature and are reconstituted periodically (usually annually), whereas Ad Hoc Committees are temporary and are dissolved once their specific task is completed.

questions.question: Which of the following statements correctly differentiates the power of the Rajya Sabha from the Lok Sabha?
questions.option_1: Rajya Sabha can initiate a motion of no-confidence against the Council of Ministers
questions.option_2: Rajya Sabha can authorise Parliament to make laws on State subjects
questions.option_3: Rajya Sabha has equal power with Lok Sabha in all financial matters
questions.option_4: Rajya Sabha can amend Money Bills
questions.solution:
Special Powers of Rajya Sabha

Under Article 249, the Rajya Sabha has a unique power to pass a resolution (by 2/3rd majority) authorising Parliament to make laws on a subject listed in the State List in the national interest. This is a distinct federal power not shared with the Lok Sabha.

questions.question: Who was the Chairman of the 'Committee to Review Policy for Public Enterprises', set up by the Government of India in 1984?
questions.solution:
Arjun Sengupta Committee (1984)

The Government of India set up a committee under the chairmanship of Arjun Sengupta in 1984 to review the policy for Public Sector Enterprises (PSEs). The committee recommended greater autonomy for PSEs and the introduction of the Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) system.
